title: Support Us Without Pause, So We May Continue to Work!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source contains deliberate boxed repetitions of Jayalalithaa's election slogan, the DMK's closing appeal, the “family politics” passage, and the 16 April 2002 Assembly resolution. They are preserved in their printed positions. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-visible salutation and repairs only unambiguous OCR defects, including the malformed date `16-4-2002`, broken names and `தந்தை பெரியார்`. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged. Contested political and financial claims remain attributed to Kalaignar's criticism and the publications or statements he quotes.
paragraphs: Only twenty-four hours remain before voting begins in the general election to the Fifteenth Legislative Assembly! Seated at the very summit of ostentation, arrogance and conceit, Jayalalithaa treated no one as worthy of regard during the past five years. No one has forgotten the self-centred tone running through every government statement she issued: “My Government,” “I announce.” The whole country watched with concern as no Opposition member was permitted in the Assembly to express a democratic difference of opinion, offer constructive criticism or raise a voice.

As soon as she assumed office after the 2011 election, Jayalalithaa said that she would meet reporters every week. That promise was never fulfilled, and the Chief Minister did not meet the press. During these five years, did she ever meet representatives of government employees' associations, farmers' associations or trade unions? She did not. Now that an election has arrived, she assumes the deceptive guise of saying, “I am for the people, I am only for the people.” But did she travel anywhere, to any district, and meet those people during the past five years? Again, she did not.

To put it plainly, instead of “I am for the people, I am only for the people,” she could say: “I am for myself, I am only for myself; I am only for Sasikala and her entourage.” If, by some misfortune, the AIADMK wins this election too, that is what she will once again say to herself. Under Rule 110, she will announce that the name “Tamil Nadu” has been changed to “Amma Nadu.”

Now that an election has arrived, she assumes the deceptive guise of saying, “I am for the people, I am only for the people.” But did she travel anywhere, to any district, and meet those people during the past five years? Again, she did not. To put it plainly, instead of “I am for the people, I am only for the people,” she could say: “I am for myself, I am only for myself; I am only for Sasikala and her entourage.” If, by some misfortune, the AIADMK wins this election too, that is what she will once again say to herself. Under Rule 110, she will announce that the name “Tamil Nadu” has been changed to “Amma Nadu.”

When she began her administration in 2011, she cited the debt incurred for the Government during the Kazhagam administration and announced that she would reduce it. In her election manifesto, Jayalalithaa said: “Tamil Nadu will be rescued from the humiliation of being a State indebted by Rs 1 lakh crore, enabling every Tamil to stand with head held high and live with self-respect.” What is the position now? Excluding the Electricity Board's debt, the Tamil Nadu Government's total debt is Rs 211,483 crore. That places a debt burden of Rs 28,778 on the head of every person in Tamil Nadu.
On 9 April 2011, a few days before the DMK administration ended, then Union Finance Minister Pranab Mukherjee praised its financial management:

> “Tamil Nadu is among the few States in India that satisfactorily maintain fiscal discipline and stability. The Tamil Nadu Government's debt is far below the recommended level. Not even for a single day has the Government obtained an overdraft from the Reserve Bank of India because its account lacked funds.”
But what do the English-language newspapers say about the condition of today's AIADMK Government?
Within ten months of the AIADMK taking office in 2011, it followed increases in milk prices and bus fares with a single, steep 37 per cent increase in electricity tariffs.

When Jayalalithaa first became Chief Minister in 2001, she raised electricity tariffs from 1 December 2001. On 15 March 2003, she again imposed tariff increases totalling Rs 1,398 crore. On that very day, she announced the cancellation of free electricity for agriculture and huts introduced by the DMK Government. She increased household tariffs by 22.13 per cent and tariffs for educational institutions by 16.61 per cent.
Even after all this, what became of Tamil Nadu's finances?
*The Hindu* itself reported: “The State Government's financial health is quite bad.”
*Junior Vikatan* wrote:

> “The Finance Department is floundering without being able to meet any target. The Government devoted its greatest attention to increasing TASMAC revenue. Had it shown the same administrative capacity in other departments, total revenue would have risen. Government lethargy is responsible. The Government has no course but to borrow in order to manage the situation. Tamil Nadu is already struggling under debt of Rs 1.8 lakh crore, and the State's overall growth will be affected. The Government has taken no firm action to collect old tax arrears or prevent tax evasion. Apart from the Tamil Nadu Government's debt, the Electricity Board owes Rs 1.5 lakh crore. Including the debts of public-sector undertakings, Tamil Nadu's total debt burden will exceed Rs 4 lakh crore.”

Jayalalithaa speaks in her statement of “family politics.” But who is Sasikala, who follows her like a shadow? Who are Ilavarasi, Sudhakaran, Dinakaran, Divakaran and Ravanan? Are those families not inseparably intertwined with Jayalalithaa? Who manages the Jazz Cinemas theatres, the MIDAS company that manufactures liquor for TASMAC, the Kodanad Estate, the Siruthavur bungalows, countless other properties and the various companies allegedly created for money transfers? Against a background burdened by all this, can Jayalalithaa speak of family politics? Does she possess any standing to do so?

At an election meeting in Thanjavur, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa praised herself: “Before making a promise, I think a thousand times. I have fulfilled the promises I made during the last Assembly election.” Her 2011 AIADMK manifesto promised:

* twenty litres of purified drinking water free of charge to every family below the poverty line; * 5.6 million new employment opportunities through that scheme; * an Electricity Security Force comprising ex-servicemen to curb power theft; * generation of 1,000 megawatts of electricity from municipal waste in 151 municipalities and corporations by 2012; * creation of ten 300-megawatt solar-energy parks by 2013, producing 3,000 megawatts; * a Singapore-style monorail system for Chennai, Madurai, Tiruchirappalli and Coimbatore; * study and implementation of a coastal road from Chennai to Kanniyakumari; and * immediate action to establish an aero park in southern Tamil Nadu.
Were all these not schemes Jayalalithaa announced after thinking a thousand times? Why were they not implemented?

Nor were these the only announcements. Successive financial statements announced a Rs 3,833.62 crore major project to restore the Cooum River; Rs 5,166 crore for river-linking and flood-prevention works; a Rs 8,350 crore monorail project on three corridors; a Rs 1,075 crore second-phase Outer Ring Road linking Nemilichery and Minjur; an integrated knowledge park at Chennai's DPI campus; and new lakes, each with a capacity of one TMC, at Thervaikandigai, Thirukandalam and Ramanjeri. What became of all these announcements? Did they fly away on the winds of publicity?

In 2012, the Government made grand announcements that memoranda of understanding would be signed with twelve companies on a single day, that they would create 36,855 jobs and that factories would be established with investments of Rs 20,925 crore. The newspapers carried the claims prominently. I asked the Government to tell the people whether the investments from those twelve companies had reached Tamil Nadu, whether they had all begun operations, and whether 36,855 people had obtained jobs. The rulers have not opened their mouths to this day. Only if something had happened could their tongues move!

At the preview conference held in Chennai on 13 February 2015 ahead of the Global Investors Meet, the Government advertised on a grand scale that agreements worth Rs 18,000 crore would be signed with seven companies. But were those agreements signed there? No.

Later, at the investors' conference conducted with Rs 100 crore of public money, did industries bringing the Rs 2.42 lakh crore investment announced by Chief Minister Jayalalithaa actually arrive? The painful answer is no.

In the statement she issued today, Jayalalithaa spoke about Sri Lankan Tamils and levelled criticism against the DMK. Does she have any standing to speak about their welfare? Has she forgotten the resolution she passed in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 16 April 2002?

> “This Legislative Assembly urges the Union Government to take immediate action so that the Sri Lankan Government arrests Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam leader Velupillai Prabhakaran at once and hands him over to the Government of India. This House further urges the Union Government not to permit any member of the banned Liberation Tigers organisation to enter the sacred land of India.”

Have Tamil Nadu's voters forgotten that Jayalalithaa introduced and passed this resolution on 16 April 2002? Jayalalithaa may have forgotten, but Tamils across the world have not. No one will be deceived by the false tears she sheds today for Sri Lankan Tamils.

My beloved Udanpirappugal, dearer to me than life itself, and respected Tamil people: as far as the Kazhagam is concerned, it is not I who contests in all 234 constituencies. Perarignar Anna himself contests; Father Periyar contests; the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam contests. I place before you my loving appeal for your wholehearted support so that a truly democratic government may dawn. Command me to work for you! We shall do what we say; we shall say only what we will do!
In a statement dated 17 January 2009, Jayalalithaa said:

> “The Sri Lankan Army does not intend to kill Sri Lankan Tamils. When a war, a battle, takes place, innocent people are killed. No country is an exception. Wherever a war or battle occurs, innocent people are killed. But what is happening in Sri Lanka today is that the Liberation Tigers are preventing Sri Lankan Tamils from moving to safe places, holding them there, and forcibly using them as shields before the Army.”
Has Jayalalithaa herself forgotten that she said this?

Next, Jayalalithaa speaks in her statement of “family politics.” But who is Sasikala, who follows her like a shadow? Who are Ilavarasi, Sudhakaran, Dinakaran, Divakaran and Ravanan? Are those families not inseparably intertwined with Jayalalithaa? Who manages the Jazz Cinemas theatres, the MIDAS company that manufactures liquor for TASMAC, the Kodanad Estate, the Siruthavur bungalows, countless other properties and the various companies allegedly created for money transfers? Against a background burdened by all this, can Jayalalithaa speak of family politics? Does she possess any standing to do so?
Has Jayalalithaa forgotten the resolution she passed in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 16 April 2002?

> “This Legislative Assembly urges the Union Government to take immediate action so that the Sri Lankan Government arrests Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam leader Velupillai Prabhakaran at once and hands him over to the Government of India. This House further urges the Union Government not to permit any member of the banned Liberation Tigers organisation to enter the sacred land of India.”

Have Tamil Nadu's voters forgotten that Jayalalithaa introduced and passed this resolution on 16 April 2002? Jayalalithaa may have forgotten, but Tamils across the world have not. No one will be deceived by the false tears she sheds today for Sri Lankan Tamils.

During the past five years, was any section of Tamil Nadu contented? Thirteen thousand people's welfare workers were dismissed merely because they had been appointed under the Kazhagam Government. They went all the way to the Supreme Court and obtained a judgment in their favour, yet the Government still had no heart to reinstate them. Is that not the mark of a heart of stone? What qualification does she then possess to call herself a “mother”?

How relentlessly were certain officials pursued and victimised over the past five years merely because they had worked efficiently in government service during the Kazhagam administration? How many people in the film industry were subjected to retaliation, and in how many ways? Films produced with Tamil titles had been granted tax exemption by the Government. Yet Jayalalithaa's Government denied even that exemption when people it disliked produced films with Tamil titles!

There are still so many excesses, acts of lawlessness, abuses of authority and injustices! Should the AIADMK Government responsible for all these cruelties be allowed to continue? The country has already become a forest in these five years; will it not be ruined into a desert? Can the people of Tamil Nadu permit it any longer? As soon as I wrote that you should regard me as contesting in all 234 constituencies, Jayalalithaa, who “copies” everything, followed me and declared in today's statement that she herself was the candidate in all 234 constituencies.

My beloved Udanpirappugal, dearer to me than life itself, and respected Tamil people: as far as the Kazhagam is concerned, it is not I who contests in all 234 constituencies. Perarignar Anna himself contests; Father Periyar contests; the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam contests; the true Dravidian movement contests.
In this election, on behalf of the Kazhagam alliance:

* the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam contests 174 seats under the Rising Sun symbol; * the Indian National Congress contests forty-one seats under the Hand symbol; * the Indian Union Muslim League contests five seats under the Ladder symbol; * the Manithaneya Makkal Katchi contests four seats under the Cup and Saucer symbol; * Puthiya Tamilagam contests four seats under the Television symbol; * the Makkal DMDK contests three seats under the Rising Sun symbol; and * the Perunthalaivar Makkal Katchi, the Tamil Nadu Farmers and Labourers Party, and the Social Equality Force Party each contest one seat under the Rising Sun symbol.

This is the moment to resolve that you will give them all your votes in overwhelming numbers, secure their victory, and dig a deep pit to bury, roots, soil and all, the lawless and tyrannical rule now prevailing in Tamil Nadu!

I place before you my loving appeal for your wholehearted support so that a truly democratic government may dawn. Command me to work for you! We shall do what we say; we shall say only what we will do!
With affection,
M.K.
